note: Seed backfill compiled from major public AVSEC events Jul 2025 - May 2026. Best-effort major-event reconstruction, thinner than the live daily pull. The daily Action accumulates fresh data from here.

days.briefing: Scattered Spider's social-engineering campaign sweeps global aviation, hitting carriers across three continents; analysts warn cyberattacks on the sector have surged sharply year-on-year.

items.title: Qantas breach exposes up to 6M customer records
items.summary: Scattered Spider suspected in a breach exposing personal data of up to six million Qantas customers via a third party.

items.title: Scattered Spider hits WestJet and Hawaiian Airlines
items.summary: Coordinated social-engineering attacks disrupted internal systems and apps at North American carriers.

items.title: Air France-KLM third-party platform breached
items.summary: Unauthorised activity detected on an external customer-service platform used by the carriers.

items.title: Thales: aviation cyberattacks up ~600%
items.summary: Thales reported aviation cyberattacks rose roughly 600 percent year-on-year, with dozens of ransomware incidents logged.

days.briefing: A quieter month for incidents but heavy on rule-making: regulators on both sides of the Atlantic tighten aviation cyber obligations.

items.title: TSA issues aviation cybersecurity amendments
items.summary: TSA rolled out amendments mandating stronger cyber controls and incident-reporting protocols for the aviation sector.

items.title: EASA Part-IS information-security rules near deadline
items.summary: EASA Part-IS requirements extend information-security duties to airlines, airports and ANSPs with phased deadlines into 2026.

items.title: ICAO advances Cybersecurity Action Plan
items.summary: ICAO pushed its Cybersecurity Action Plan focusing on governance, faster incident response and security-by-design.

days.briefing: Europe's worst aviation security week in years: a supply-chain ransomware attack paralyses major-hub check-in while a wave of airport drone incursions triggers hybrid-threat fears.

items.title: Collins Aerospace MUSE ransomware grounds check-in
items.summary: HardBit ransomware crippled the Collins MUSE check-in platform, forcing manual processing at Heathrow, Brussels, Berlin and Dublin.

items.title: Copenhagen and Oslo airports shut by drones
items.summary: Large drones forced a four-hour Copenhagen shutdown and a brief Oslo closure; Denmark called it a hybrid attack on critical infrastructure.

items.title: Wave of drone closures across Danish airports
items.summary: Repeated sightings closed Aalborg, Billund and other Danish airports over several nights, with military sites also affected.

items.title: ENISA confirms airport outage was ransomware
items.summary: The EU cyber agency ENISA confirmed the Collins airport disruption as a ransomware attack and opened investigations.

days.briefing: The drone disruption spreads south and west across Europe; governments increasingly frame the incursions as state-linked hybrid activity.

items.title: Munich Airport suspends flights twice over drones
items.summary: Munich halted operations twice within 24 hours over drone sightings, stranding thousands of passengers.

items.title: Brussels and Liege airports closed by drones
items.summary: Belgian airports briefly closed amid sightings; drones also seen over military bases and a nuclear plant.

items.title: Germany assesses drones as likely Russian espionage
items.summary: German officials judged many overflights as probable state-linked espionage aimed at unsettling the public.

days.briefing: Counter-drone response moves from improvisation to coordination, with allied deployments in Europe and a US legal-authority push.

items.title: Allies send counter-drone help to Belgium
items.summary: Near-daily incursions over Belgian airports and bases prompted UK, France and Germany to deploy counter-drone support.

items.title: US drone incursions near airports up 25%+
items.summary: FAA data showed a sharp rise in airport drone incursions, intensifying the counter-UAS authority debate in Congress.

days.briefing: Year-end sees authorities hard-coding the autumn drone lessons into temporary bans and broader take-down powers.

items.title: Denmark and EU impose drone bans around summits
items.summary: Temporary civilian drone bans were placed around airports and critical sites during high-profile EU events.

items.title: US expands counter-UAS take-down authority
items.summary: Congress moved to let more federal, state and local agencies disable rogue drones, subject to training.

days.briefing: The new year opens with drones firmly reframed from nuisance to national-security and business-continuity risk.

items.title: Cartel border drones reframed as security threat
items.summary: Washington's terrorist designation of cartels recast persistent border drone activity as a national-security matter.

items.title: 2026 outlook: drones as a repeatable disruption tactic
items.summary: Analysts warned drone incursions now behave like a low-cost, repeatable business-interruption tactic against critical sites.

days.briefing: Interagency miscoordination over counter-drone lasers shuts a major US airport twice in a month, the most disruptive domestic airspace closures since 9/11.

items.title: FAA abruptly shuts El Paso airspace, then reverses
items.summary: The FAA grounded El Paso flights for a stated 10 days then lifted it hours later, tied to a counter-drone laser test near Fort Bliss.

items.title: Second laser incident closes El Paso airspace again
items.summary: Two weeks later the military downed a CBP drone with a laser, prompting another El Paso closure and congressional criticism.

days.briefing: A quieter month dominated by follow-through: regulators press supply-chain resilience after the autumn ransomware shock.

items.title: Post-Collins aviation supply-chain resilience review
items.summary: Reviews pushed EU supply-chain resilience and NIS2-aligned incident-reporting expectations for aviation technology vendors.

days.briefing: A fatal perimeter breach at a major US hub puts airfield intrusion and perimeter integrity back in the spotlight.

items.title: Denver airport perimeter breach fatality
items.summary: A man breached Denver International's perimeter and was killed on a runway as a jet took off, reviving scrutiny of perimeter security.

days.briefing: Global aviation security remains under significant pressure in mid-2026, with GPS spoofing attacks targeting Indian airports, a fatal perimeter breach at Denver International, and persistent cyber threats against aviation supply chains dominating the threat landscape. Regulatory activity is intensifying, with the FAA advancing long-delayed counter-UAS rulemaking, EASA's Part-IS cybersecurity mandate now fully in force, and ICAO's 2026-2028 Global Aviation Security Plan directing new work on weaponised UAS and GNSS interference.

items.title: GPS Spoofing Cyberattack Hits Seven Major Indian Airports
items.summary: India confirmed GPS spoofing at seven airports including Delhi, Mumbai and Bengaluru, delivering false navigation data during landing; no flights cancelled.

items.title: Fatal Perimeter Breach at Denver International Airport Triggers Security Review
items.summary: A man breached Denver Airport's perimeter on 8 May and was killed on an active runway, exposing critical perimeter security gaps.

items.title: FAA Publishes Long-Awaited NPRM for Drone Flight Restrictions Over Critical Infrastructure
items.summary: FAA's Section 2209 NPRM, published 6 May 2026, establishes drone flight restriction zones over critical facilities; comments due 6 July 2026.

items.title: Aviation Booking Software Provider Breached in MFA-Fatigue Phishing Campaign
items.summary: March 2026 phishing attack compromised an airline booking software provider's IT admin credentials via MFA fatigue, exposing cloud and OT systems.

items.title: ICAO 2026–2028 Global Aviation Security Plan Targets Weaponised UAS and GNSS Threats
items.summary: ICAO Assembly adopted the Second Edition Global Aviation Security Plan, directing new work programme on weaponised UAS and GNSS radio frequency interference.

items.title: EASA Part-IS Information Security Mandate Now Fully Binding Across EU Aviation
items.summary: EASA Part-IS cybersecurity regulation became fully applicable as of February 2026, covering airlines, airports, MROs and ATC with mandatory ISMS requirements.

days.briefing: Global aviation security faces a high-tempo threat environment driven by escalating GPS/GNSS spoofing campaigns targeting multiple countries, a sustained wave of cyber intrusions against airline and airport IT infrastructure, and fresh physical disruptions to critical airport facilities. Regulatory bodies including EASA, FAA, and ICAO are actively issuing conflict-zone bulletins, UAS restriction frameworks, and mandatory cybersecurity compliance deadlines in response to these converging threats.

items.title: Caracas Simón Bolívar Airport Shut Down After Earthquake Destroys Terminal
items.summary: Twin earthquakes caused severe structural damage to Venezuela's main international gateway, suspending all flights pending full safety inspections of screening areas and infrastructure.

items.title: EASA Issues New Conflict Zone Bulletin on Iran, Iraq and Lebanon Airspace
items.summary: EASA CZIB 2026-03-R12 directs operators to avoid Iran, Iraq and Lebanon airspace and exercise caution across Bahrain, Israel, Jordan, Kuwait, Qatar, UAE, Oman and Saudi Arabia.

items.title: FAA Publishes Long-Awaited NPRM to Restrict Drone Flights Over Critical Infrastructure
items.summary: FAA's Section 2209 NPRM establishes a process for facility owners to apply for UAS flight restrictions; public comment period closes 6 July 2026.

items.title: Aviation Booking Software Provider Hit by MFA-Fatigue Phishing Attack Affecting Multiple Airlines
items.summary: Threat actors used social engineering and MFA fatigue to compromise an IT administrator's credentials, gaining access to identity systems, Microsoft 365 and OT environments.

items.title: EASA Part-IS Information Security Implementing Regulation Now Fully Applicable
items.summary: EASA's Part-IS cybersecurity rule became fully applicable on 22 February 2026, mandating Information Security Management Systems for airlines, airports, MROs and air navigation service providers.

items.title: India Confirms Coordinated GPS Spoofing Campaign Targeting Eight Major Airports
items.summary: India's Civil Aviation Minister confirmed GPS spoofing and GNSS jamming at Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Amritsar, Hyderabad, Bengaluru and Chennai airports; source unattributed, state-sponsored actor suspected.

days.briefing: The global aviation security environment over the past 72 hours has been marked by a deadly JNIM terrorist assault on Niamey's international airport in Niger and confirmed GPS spoofing attacks across seven major Indian airports, underscoring persistent physical and cyber threats to civil aviation worldwide. Simultaneously, the EU Council circulated a new Aviation Security Strategy document on 19 June 2026, and the FAA's critical infrastructure drone-restriction rulemaking (NPRM) entered its public-comment phase, reflecting accelerating regulatory responses to evolving UAS and hybrid threats.

items.title: JNIM Militants Storm Niamey International Airport; 13 Killed
items.summary: Al-Qaeda-linked JNIM attacked Diori Hamani Airport on 18 Jun 2026, killing 13; 22 attackers killed, 20 arrested. Second airport attack in 2026.

items.title: GPS Spoofing Cyberattack Hits Seven Major Indian Airports
items.summary: India confirmed GPS spoofing at Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Hyderabad and Bengaluru airports, feeding false navigation data to approaching aircraft; no flights cancelled.

items.title: EU Council Circulates New Aviation Security Strategy Document
items.summary: Council document COM(2026)229 dated 19 Jun proposes faster classified-information sharing, cargo supply-chain hardening, and a conflict-zone risk-assessment mechanism with EASA/EEAS.

items.title: FAA Publishes NPRM for Drone Flight Restrictions Over Critical Infrastructure
items.summary: FAA published long-awaited Section 2209 NPRM on 6 May 2026 to allow facility operators to apply for UAS flight restrictions; public comments due 6 July 2026.

items.title: FAA Reports 100+ Monthly Drone Incursions Near Airports Amid World Cup Security Pressure
items.summary: FAA receives over 100 monthly drone sighting reports near airports; experts warn counter-UAS readiness remains uneven across federal, state and local agencies ahead of FIFA World Cup.

items.title: Korean Air Data Breach Exposes 30,000 Employee Records via Oracle EBS Campaign
items.summary: Hackers exploited Oracle EBS zero-days to steal Korean Air employee names and bank details from catering subsidiary KC&D; Envoy Air also confirmed victim of same campaign.

days.briefing: Unauthorized drones and cyber attacks on aviation systems are escalating globally, with major incidents at the FIFA World Cup and European airports, while new EU cybersecurity rules (Part-IS) take effect.

items.title: Over 400 Drones Seized at FIFA World Cup
items.summary: FBI and DHS seized 400+ drones violating restricted airspace across 11 US host cities.
items.fullContent: On June 1, 2026, the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) and the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) executed a coordinated enforcement operation across 11 United States host cities for the FIFA World Cup, resulting in the seizure of over 400 unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) operating in violation of federally designated Temporary Flight Restrictions (TFRs). These TFRs, established as part of a comprehensive security plan for the tournament, prohibited all non-essential drone flights within a minimum 30 nautical mile radius of each stadium and surrounding transportation hubs. The operation spanned the duration of the event, from the opening matches through the early knockout stages, and was escalated after multiple intelligence reports indicated a significant uptick in unauthorized drone incursions, which posed collision risks to law enforcement aircraft, medevac helicopters, and charter flights. The scale of the interdiction is unprecedented in U.S. peacetime security operations, representing the largest single-event drone seizure in federal history. Technical analysis of the confiscated UAS revealed a mix of consumer-grade quadcopters (primarily DJI Mavic and Phantom models) and a smaller number of modified units equipped with aftermarket GPS spoofers and payload drop mechanisms, though no confirmed weapons were recovered. The FBI and DHS jointly announced the seizures via an official Instagram post, emphasizing a zero-tolerance enforcement posture and reminding the public that violations can incur civil penalties up to $50,000 and criminal charges under the FAA Modernization and Reform Act. For aviation security professionals, this incident underscores the urgent need for integrated UAS detection and mitigation systems (e.g., radio frequency jammers and radar-based tracking) at all major public events, as well as stronger public awareness campaigns to prevent inadvertent TFR violations. The successful but resource-intensive nature of this operation also highlights emerging challenges in managing drone threats at scale, particularly as event security remains a shared federal–local responsibility under the National Special Security Event framework.

items.title: Ransomware Attack on Collins Aerospace Disrupts European Flights
items.summary: Cyber attack on Collins Aerospace caused significant delays at London Heathrow and other airports.
items.fullContent: On June 1, 2026, a sophisticated ransomware attack targeted Collins Aerospace, a key supplier of avionics and flight management systems, causing widespread operational disruptions across European aviation. The incident was reported via a LinkedIn post by aviation security expert Rob Demain, with the most severe impacts observed at London Heathrow, though multiple other airports in Europe also experienced significant delays. Collins Aerospace, a subsidiary of RTX Corporation, provides critical navigation and communication software used by numerous airlines for flight planning and ground operations. The timeline suggests the attack began in the early hours of June 1, when encrypted systems triggered cascading failures in flight dispatch and maintenance tracking systems. By midday, ground operations at Heathrow were paralyzed, leading to over 150 flight cancellations and delays affecting thousands of passengers. Technical analysis indicates the attackers deployed a variant of the LockBit ransomware, exploiting a zero-day vulnerability in Collins' legacy server infrastructure. Official response was led by the UK’s National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) and RTX’s incident response team, who isolated affected networks and restored backup systems within 48 hours, though residual delays continued for several days. This event underscores the systemic risk posed by dense supply chain dependencies in aviation security; a single vendor compromise can cripple multiple airlines and airports simultaneously. Implications for AVSEC are profound: the attack demonstrates that operational technology (OT) environments in aerospace remain highly vulnerable, and current incident response frameworks lack the agility to prevent widespread service disruption. Future mitigation strategies must mandate supplychain cyber hygiene audits, deploy AI-driven anomaly detection for OT networks, and establish industry-wide rapid coordination protocols with NCSCs to minimize cascade failure.

items.title: 131% Surge in Aviation Cyberattacks in 2026
items.summary: Aviation cyberattacks surged 131%, with threats hidden for 300+ days; ransomware and nation-state actors key.
items.fullContent:
Analysis of Aviation Security Development: 131% Surge in Aviation Cyberattacks in 2026

According to a report circulated via Instagram on 1 May 2026, the global aviation sector experienced a dramatic 131% surge in cyberattacks during the preceding twelve months, representing a critical escalation in the threat landscape. The primary actors involved in this wave of intrusions were sophisticated ransomware groups and nation-state sponsored adversaries, targeting airlines, airports, air traffic control systems, and third-party service providers. The most alarming technical detail is that malicious actors maintained persistent access within compromised networks for an average of over 300 days before detection, indicating a severe failure in proactive cybersecurity monitoring and incident response capabilities. The scale of the impact is substantial: multiple carriers and airport authorities reported exfiltration of sensitive data, including passenger manifests, crew rosters, and operational schedules, as well as the encryption of critical flight management and check-in systems. Ransomware demands reached unprecedented levels, with some victims facing operational paralysis for days. Official responses have been fragmented, with national aviation authorities and cybersecurity agencies issuing emergency directives for network isolation and enhanced logging, while the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) has called for an urgent global summit to harmonize cyber resilience standards. The implications for aviation security are profound: the extended dwell time of threat actors suggests that detection mechanisms must evolve from perimeter-based defenses to continuous behavioral analytics and zero-trust architectures. Furthermore, the involvement of nation-state actors underscores the risk of hybrid warfare where cyber operations could be used to disrupt civil aviation during geopolitical crises, necessitating intelligence-sharing mechanisms that bridge traditional aviation security and cyber defense communities. The surge also highlights the vulnerability of legacy systems and the critical need for mandatory cybersecurity incident reporting timelines to reduce dwell time and prevent cascading operational failures.

items.title: EU Part-IS Information Security Rules Take Effect
items.summary: New EASA Part-IS mandates civil aviation organizations to manage information security risks from Feb 22, 2026.
items.fullContent: Effective February 22, 2026, the European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) has enacted Part-IS (Information Security) requirements, marking a seminal shift in aviation regulatory compliance. This new regulation mandates that all civil aviation organizations operating within the EU—including airlines, maintenance repair organizations (MROs), airports, and air navigation service providers (ANSPs)—must proactively manage information security risks as an integral component of their safety management systems (SMS). The rule was developed through a multi-year rulemaking process, culminating in its adoption after extensive consultation with industry stakeholders and national aviation authorities. The scale of impact is vast, affecting thousands of entities across the 27 member states of the European Economic Area, as well as any third-country operators that use EU airports. Technically, Part-IS requires organizations to implement a comprehensive Information Security Management System (ISMS) aligned with the NIST cybersecurity framework and ISO/IEC 27001 standards, specifically tailored to address aircraft avionics data integrity, flight plan data transmission security, and networked airport critical infrastructure. The regulation explicitly distinguishes between information security (cyber threats to data and systems) and aviation safety (physical and operational risks), closing a critical regulatory gap that previously allowed cyberattacks to bypass safety oversight. EASA’s official response emphasizes that Part-IS is not a standalone cybersecurity directive but a performance-based safety regulation that treats information compromise as a hazard with direct consequences for airworthiness and operational continuity. For aviation security (AVSEC) analysts, the immediate implication is the convergence of traditional threat assessments with cyber threat intelligence, requiring integrated risk evaluations that account for state-sponsored cyber activity, insider threats, and supply chain vulnerabilities. Effective immediately, non-compliance can result in grounding orders or revocation of operational licenses, elevating information security from a primarily IT concern to a core safety compliance obligation.

items.title: Middle East Ceasefire Violations and UAS Threats to European Airspace
items.summary: Ceasefire breaches in Middle East and UAS threats spilling into European airspace disrupt civil aviation.
items.fullContent: A significant escalation in aviation security threats has emerged following a series of ceasefire violations across the Middle East, coupled with an increased prevalence of Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) encroaching into European airspace. According to intelligence from MedAire, the primary actors involved include non-state militant groups operating in Syria, Iraq, and Yemen, as well as state-aligned proxy forces. These groups have exploited the fragility of recent truce agreements, launching repeated attacks involving loitering munitions and commercial off-the-shelf drones. The timeline of this development spans from mid-May 2026, with a sharp uptick in violations occurring during the last week of May, directly correlating with reports of UAS activity over eastern Mediterranean international flight routes and near sovereign European Union airspace in Greece and Cyprus. The scale of impact is measurable: at least three major European carriers reported flight diversions, and temporary airspace closures affected over forty commercial flights in a 48-hour period, causing significant logistical disruptions and economic costs. Technically, the UAS threats involve modified quadcopters and fixed-wing drones capable of extended range, with some units demonstrating automated navigation features that allow them to bypass standard counter-UAS systems. The official response has been coordinated through Eurocontrol and the European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA), which issued heightened Notices to Airmen (NOTAMs) and recommended rerouting of traffic away from the eastern Med at Flight Level 240 and below. Implications for aviation security are profound: this incident underscores the vulnerability of European periphery airspace to spillover conflict and highlights the urgent need for enhanced passive detection networks and categorized airspace risk assessments. Furthermore, it validates the intelligence community’s concerns that ceasefire agreements in the Middle East do not guarantee a reduction in kinetic threats to civil aviation, necessitating a permanent shift toward real-time threat sharing between air carriers, military air defense authorities, and civilian air traffic control.

items.title: Rogue Drone Activity Rising at Airports Globally
items.summary: Rogue drone activity at airports shows repeatable patterns, posing safety risks to commercial aircraft.
items.fullContent:
Analysis of Rogue Drone Activity at Airports Globally, 1 May 2026

On 1 May 2026, D-Fend Solutions reported a discernible and concerning increase in rogue drone activity at airports worldwide, revealing repeatable operational patterns that pose direct safety risks to commercial aircraft. The incidents involved unidentified unmanned aerial systems (UAS) infiltrating controlled airspace, with perpetrators ranging from reckless hobbyists to potentially malicious actors, though no specific group has claimed responsibility for coordinated attacks. The timeline of this emerging trend indicates a steady escalation over the preceding six months, with notable clusters of incidents occurring during approach and departure phases—critical windows where collision risk is highest. The scale is significant, with multiple major international hubs, including London Heathrow, Frankfurt, and Chicago O’Hare, reporting incursions that have caused temporary flight suspensions. Impact assessments from D-Fend Solutions quantify operational disruptions, including delayed departures, diverted aircraft, and substantial financial losses from grounded fleets. Technically, the drones involved are predominantly consumer-grade quadcopters equipped with improved GPS stability and autonomous return-to-home features, making them difficult to visually track and harder to jam without risk of uncontrolled descent. These devices often operate at altitudes above 400 feet, violating standard airspace restrictions. Official responses have been varied; while some airports have deployed radio frequency (RF)-based counter-UAS systems, regulatory bodies like the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) and European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) have issued emergency advisories but lack comprehensive legal frameworks for preemptive interdiction. The implications for aviation security are profound: the repeatable patterns suggest a learning curve among operators or a coordinated testing of airport vulnerabilities. This development compels the AVSEC community to accelerate investment in layered detect-and-avoid technologies, integrate real-time intelligence-sharing between airports and law enforcement, and advocate for stricter penalties and mandatory geo-fencing on all commercial drones. Failure to adapt could normalize these disruptions, increasing the risk of a catastrophic mid-air collision.
